name: Yau Method (4x4)
description: The dominant 4x4 speedsolving method, developed by Robert Yau. It builds on the reduction approach but reorders the steps to allow efficient cross building and better lookahead. By solving three cross edges first, then centers, then the final cross edge alongside edge pairing, solvers can transition seamlessly into a 3x3-stage CFOP finish. Yau is used by virtually all top 4x4 competitors worldwide.

steps.name: First 3 Cross Edges
steps.description: Pair and place 3 of the 4 cross dedges (double-edges) into their correct positions on the bottom face. Leaving one cross edge unsolved preserves a free slice for efficient center building.
tips: Plan at least 2 cross edges in inspection
Use wide moves to pair edges without disrupting others
Color neutrality helps find easier solutions

steps.name: 3 F2L Centers
steps.description: Build the centers for 3 of the faces used in F2L (the non-cross face centers are solved later). The free slice left by the missing cross edge makes center commutators easier.
tips: Solve opposite centers first for better efficiency
Use the free slice to maneuver pieces
Plan center solutions during edge pairing

steps.name: Last Cross Edge + Last Centers
steps.description: Pair and insert the final cross edge while completing the remaining centers. This step closes the freedom of the cube and transitions into edge pairing.
tips: Try to solve the last cross edge and final centers simultaneously
Keep track of which centers are unsolved
Avoid disrupting already-completed cross edges

steps.name: 3-2-3 Edge Pairing
steps.description: Pair all remaining dedges using the 3-2-3 technique: place 3 unpaired edges in a slice, pair them with a slice move, then repeat with 2, then 3 more edges. This is the most efficient edge pairing strategy for 4x4.
tips: Learn the 3-2-3 cycle structure thoroughly
Look ahead to the next set of edges while pairing
Minimize slice moves and regrips
Practice recognizing edge pieces quickly across all faces

steps.name: F2L (First Two Layers)
steps.description: With the cube now reduced to a 3x3 state and the cross already solved, complete the first two layers by pairing and inserting corner-edge pairs into their slots, exactly as in CFOP.
tips: The cross is already done, so start F2L immediately
Use standard CFOP F2L algorithms and intuition
Watch for OLL and PLL parity setups

steps.name: OLL (Orientation of Last Layer)
steps.description: Orient all last layer pieces so the top face is a solid color. On 4x4, there is a chance of encountering OLL parity, which requires an additional algorithm.
tips: Learn to recognize OLL parity early
OLL parity algorithm adds ~12 moves when it occurs
Standard 3x3 OLL algorithms apply when no parity

steps.name: PLL (Permutation of Last Layer)
steps.description: Permute all last layer pieces to their correct positions. PLL parity can also occur on 4x4, requiring an additional algorithm to correct.
tips: PLL parity occurs about 50% of the time on 4x4
Learn a fast PLL parity algorithm early
Standard 3x3 PLL algorithms apply when no parity

pros: Efficient cross construction built into the method
Smooth transition from edge pairing into 3x3 stage
cons: Center building with a free slot requires practice
3-2-3 edge pairing has a significant learning curve
OLL and PLL parity algorithms are long and disruptive
creator: Robert Yau
